title: Calculating and interpreting residuals
description: Practice calculating residuals in scatterplots and interpreting what they measure.

assessment_items.question_content:
Anna set up a lemonade stand on her block over the summer. She recorded each day’s high temperature and the number of cups of lemonade she sold for $10$ days. 

After plotting her results, Anna noticed that the relationship between her two variables was fairly linear, so she used her data to calculate the following least squares regression equation for predicting lemonade sales, in cups, from the daily high temperature, in degrees Fahrenheit:

$\hat y = -34 +\dfrac{3}{5} x$

**What is the residual if a day had a high temperature of $95$ degrees and Anna sold $21$ cups of lemonade?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] cups
hints:
##What is a residual?##

Residuals are errors. More specifically, they are the differences between the observed value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{observed }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

##Calculating the predicted value

We can predict the lemonade sales for a $95$ degree day using the least squares regression line like this:

$\begin{align}
\hat y &= -34 +\dfrac{3}{5}(95) \\
&=23
\end{align}$

answers.message: Be careful with the order of subtraction. A residual is always the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.
You found the predicted y-value, but not the residual. The residual is the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joe sells used cars. He recorded the age (in years) of each car on his lot along with the number of kilometers it had been driven. After plotting his results, Joe noticed that the relationship between the two variables was fairly linear, so he used the data to calculate the following least squares regression equation for predicting distance driven from the age of the car:

$\hat{y} = 11{,}000+18{,}000x$

**What is the residual of a car that is $2$ years old and has been driven $50{,}000\text{ km}$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{km}$
hints:
##What is a residual?##

Residuals are errors. More specifically, they are the differences between the observed value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{observed }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

##Calculating the predicted value

We can predict the distance driven for a $2$ year old car using the least squares regression line like this:

$\begin{align}
\hat y &= 11{,}000+18{,}000(2) \\\\
&=47{,}000
\end{align}$

answers.message: Be careful with the order of subtraction. A residual is always the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.
You found the predicted y-value, but not the residual. The residual is the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.

assessment_items.question_content:
Vera rents bicycles to tourists. She recorded the height (in $\text{cm}$) of each customer and the frame size (in $\text{cm}$) of the bicycle that customer rented. 

After plotting her results, Vera noticed that the relationship between the two variables was fairly linear, so she used the data to calculate the following least squares regression equation for predicting bicycle frame size from the height of the customer:

$\hat{y} = \dfrac{1}{3}+\dfrac{1}{3}x$

**What is the residual of a customer with a height of $155\,\text{cm}$ who rents a bike with a $51\,\text{cm}$ frame?**

[http://www.wheelbase.co.uk/size-chart/]: (Source)

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
##What is a residual?##

Residuals are errors. More specifically, they are the differences between the observed value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{observed }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

##Calculating the predicted value

We can predict the frame size for a $155$ $\text{cm}$ tall customer using the least squares regression line like this:

$\begin{align}
\hat y &= \dfrac{1}{3} +\dfrac{1}{3}(155) \\\\
&=52
\end{align}$

answers.message: Be careful with the order of subtraction. A residual is always the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.
You found the predicted y-value, but not the residual. The residual is the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.

assessment_items.question_content:
Danila breeds peregrine falcons. She recorded the breadth (in $\text{mm}$) of each egg and the mass (in $\text{g}$) of the falcon chick that hatched from it.

After plotting her results, Danila noticed that the relationship between the two variables was fairly linear, so she used the data to calculate the following least squares regression equation for predicting the mass of the chick from the breadth of the egg:

$\hat{y} =-47+2x$

**What is the residual of a $29\,\text{g}$ falcon chick who hatches from an egg with a breadth of  $40\,\text{mm}$ ?**

[http://scholarworks.boisestate.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1177&context=bio_facpubs]: (Source)

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{g}$
hints:
##What is a residual?##

Residuals are errors. More specifically, they are the differences between the observed value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{observed }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

##Calculating the predicted value

We can predict the chick mass for a $40$ $\text{mm}$ wide egg using the least squares regression line like this:

$\begin{align}
\hat y &= -47 +2(40) \\
&=33
\end{align}$

answers.message: Be careful with the order of subtraction. A residual is always the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.
You found the predicted y-value, but not the residual. The residual is the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alexander uses cupric chloride to etch circuit boards. He recorded the room temperature, in $^\circ\text{C}$, and the etching rate, in $\dfrac{\mu\text{m}}{\text{min}}$, of the cupric chloride.

After plotting his results, Alexander noticed that the relationship between the two variables was fairly linear, so he used the data to calculate the following least squares regression equation for predicting the etching rate from the room temperature:

$\hat y = 2 +\dfrac{1}{5} x$

**What is the residual if the room temperature was $25^\circ\text{C}$ and the cupric chloride had an etching rate of $5\dfrac{\mu\text{m}}{\text{min}}$ ?**

[http://techref.massmind.org/Techref/pcb/etch/cucl2.htm]: (Source)

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\dfrac{\mu\text{m}}{\text{min}}$
hints:
##What is a residual?##

Residuals are errors. More specifically, they are the differences between the observed value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{observed }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

##Calculating the predicted value

We can predict the etching rate for a $25$ $^\circ\text{C}$ room using the least squares regression line like this:

$\begin{align}
\hat y &= 2 +\dfrac{1}{5}(25) \\\\
&=7
\end{align}$

answers.message: Be careful with the order of subtraction. A residual is always the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.
You found the predicted y-value, but not the residual. The residual is the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.

assessment_items.question_content:
The theater director recorded the number of seats occupied at past performances and the total revenue per performance.

After plotting her results, the director noticed that the relationship between the two variables was fairly linear, so she used the data to calculate the following least squares regression equation for predicting the total revenue, in dollars, from the number of seats occupied.

$\hat y = -325+14 x$

**What is the residual of a performance with a revenue of $\$700$ and $70$ seats occupied ?**

$\$$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##What is a residual?##

Residuals are errors. More specifically, they are the differences between the observed value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{observed }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

##Calculating the predicted value

We can predict the revenue for a performance with $70$ seats occupied using the least squares regression line like this:

$\begin{align}
\hat y &= -325 +14(70) \\
&=655
\end{align}$

answers.message: Be careful with the order of subtraction. A residual is always the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.
You found the predicted y-value, but not the residual. The residual is the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.

assessment_items.question_content:
The fuel consortium chairman at an airport recorded the cost per barrel of crude oil and the cost per barrel of jet fuel each week.

After plotting his results, the chairman noticed that the relationship between the two variables was fairly linear, so he used the data to calculate the following least squares regression equation for predicting the cost, in dollars, per barrel of jet fuel from the cost, in dollars, per barrel of crude oil.

$\hat y = 2+\dfrac{7}{6}x$

[http://marketrealist.com/2014/09/why-political-and-legal-factors-impact-airline-industry/]: (Source)

**What is the residual of a week in which a barrel of crude oil costs $\$60$ and a barrel of jet fuel costs $\$78$ ?**

$\$$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##What is a residual?##

Residuals are errors. More specifically, they are the differences between the observed value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{observed }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

##Calculating the predicted value

We can predict the cost of jet fuel when a barrel of crude oil costs $\$60$ using the least squares regression line like this:

$\begin{align}
\hat y &= 2 +\dfrac{7}{6}(60) \\
&=72
\end{align}$

##Calculating the residual

$\begin{align}
\text{residual}&=\text{observed}-\text{predicted}\\\\\\
&=\$78 -\$72 \\\\\\
&=\$6
\end{align}$

answers.message: Be careful with the order of subtraction. A residual is always the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.
You found the predicted y-value, but not the residual. The residual is the observed y-value minus the predicted y-value.

assessment_items.question_content:
A cooking competition rated each participant's dish on both appearance and taste according to a $10$ point scale. An approximate least-squares regression line was used to predict the taste rating from a given appearance rating.

![A graph plots Taste rating, from 0 to 10, in increments of 1, versus Appearance rating, from 0 to 10, in increments of 1, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (1, 2), (2, 8), (4, 3), (6, 7), and (8, 8). A line of regression rises through (0, 3), (4, 5), and (8, 7). A green arrow, points vertically upward from the line to (2, 8). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0e0072da2c061b64b371c4edf095ec3e5e93d001)

**Interpret the residual for the dish indicated in the scatterplot above.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##What is a residual?

Residuals are errors.  More specifically, they are the differences between the actual value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{actual }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

We can see visually that the indicated point is $4$ units above the least-squares regression line.

![A graph plots Taste rating, from 0 to 10, in increments of 1, versus Appearance rating, from 0 to 10, in increments of 1, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (1, 2), (2, 8), (4, 3), (6, 7), and (8, 8). A line of regression rises through (0, 3), (4, 5), and (8, 7). A green arrow, labeled 4, points vertically upward from the line to (2, 8). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/65c7bb0d8fa5310c4d8de281783a17a124ce31d6)

This vertical distance describes how far away the actual taste rating was from this dish's predicted taste rating.

Since the point is above the line, the dish's taste rating was higher than the line predicted based on its appearance.
This dish's actual taste rating was $4$ points higher than predicted based on its appearance.

choices.content: This dish's actual taste rating was $4$ points lower than predicted based on its appearance.
This dish's actual taste rating was $4$ points higher than predicted based on its appearance.
This dish's actual appearance rating was $4$ points lower than predicted based on its taste.
This dish's actual appearance rating was $4$ points higher than predicted based on its taste.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of young adults reported their average number of hours of exercise per week and measured their resting heart rates. An approximate least-squares regression line was used to predict the resting heart rate from a given number of hours of exercise per week.

![A graph plots Resting heart rate, in beats per minute, from 60 to 92, in increments of 4, versus Hours of exercise per week, from 0 to 18, in increments of 1, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (0, 91), (1, 85), (2.5, 79), (5, 72), (8, 78), (9, 80), (10, 73), (14, 66), and (18, 64). A line of regression falls through (0, 86), (6, 78), and (18, 62). A green arrow, points vertically upward from the line to (9, 80). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/13705c58289101d81ea8578d8b168cd97736e537)

**Interpret the residual for the young adult indicated in the scatterplot above.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##What is a residual?

Residuals are errors.  More specifically, they are the differences between the actual value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{actual }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

We can see visually that the indicated point is $6$ units above the least-squares regression line.

![A graph plots Resting heart rate, in beats per minute, from 60 to 92, in increments of 4, versus Hours of exercise per week, from 0 to 18, in increments of 1, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (0, 91), (1, 85), (2.5, 79), (5, 72), (8, 78), (9, 80), (10, 73), (14, 66), and (18, 64). A line of regression falls through (0, 86), (6, 78), and (18, 62). A green arrow, labeled 6, points vertically upward from the line to (9, 80). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1e77bfc8c4fe4a8f23b5e718f169fb2446d8e2a3)

This vertical distance describes how far away the actual resting heart rate was from this young adult's predicted resting heart rate.

Since the point is above the line, the young adult's resting heart rate was higher than the line predicted based on the young adult's number of hours of exercise per week.
This young adult's resting heart rate was $6$ more beats per minute than predicted based on the number of hours of exercise.

choices.content: This young adult exercises for $6$ more hours than predicted based on the resting heart rate.
This young adult exercises for $6$ fewer hours than predicted based on the resting heart rate.
This young adult's resting heart rate was $6$ more beats per minute than predicted based on the number of hours of exercise.
This young adult's resting heart rate was $6$ fewer beats per minute than predicted based on the number of hours of exercise.

assessment_items.question_content:
Zhang Lei creates and sells wreaths. On her website, she gives the diameter, in inches, and weight, in pounds, of each wreath. An approximate least-squares regression line was used to predict the weight from a given diameter.

![A graph plots Weight, in pounds, from 0 to 12, in increments of 1, versus Diameter of wreath, in inches, from 16 to 40, in increments of 5, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (18, 2.5), (18, 3), (24, 4), (30, 8), (36, 8), and (40, 13). A line of regression rises through (16, 2), (26, 6), and (36, 10). A green arrow, points vertically downward from the line to (36, 8). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0c841a119eaf30110efbf3c2d779c76e34207e9a)

**Interpret the residual for the wreath indicated in the scatterplot above.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##What is a residual?

Residuals are errors.  More specifically, they are the differences between the actual value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{actual }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

We can see visually that the indicated point is $2$ units below the least-squares regression line.

![A graph plots Weight, in pounds, from 0 to 12, in increments of 1, versus Diameter of wreath, in inches, from 16 to 40, in increments of 5, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (18, 2.5), (18, 3), (24, 4), (30, 8), (36, 8), and (40, 13). A line of regression rises through (16, 2), (26, 6), and (36, 10). A red arrow, labeled negative 2, points vertically downward from the line to (36, 8). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0635759b495cb3e768764b6d694ed80609bb2404)

This vertical distance describes how far away the actual weight was from this wreath's predicted weight.

Since the point is below the line, the wreath's weight was lower than the line predicted based on the wreath's diameter.
This wreath weighs $2$ fewer pounds than predicted based on the diameter.

choices.content: This wreath weighs $2$ more pounds than predicted based on the diameter.
This wreath weighs $2$ fewer pounds than predicted based on the diameter.
This wreath has a diameter $2$ inches greater than predicted based on the weight.
This wreath has a diameter $2$ inches less than predicted based on the weight.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cadan tracked the storage life of bunches of bananas in his store and the storage temperature of the area where he displayed them. An approximate least-squares regression line was used to predict the storage life from a given storage temperature.

![A graph plots Storage life, in days, from 0 to 11, in increments of 1, versus Storage temperature, in degrees Celsius, from 14 to 20, in increments of 1, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (14, 8), (14, 9.5), (15, 10.5), (16, 6), (16, 9.5), (17, 7), (17, 9), (18, 5.5), (18, 7), (19, 4.9), (20, 4.8), and (20, 5.5). A line of regression falls through (10, 11), (16, 8), and (20, 5). A green arrow, points vertically downward from the line to (16, 6). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c91cc3cfba46f22226a795c7c1a976ffedfac316)

[http://www.fspublishers.org/published_papers/87164_..pdf]: (Source) 

**Interpret the residual for the bunch indicated in the scatterplot above.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##What is a residual?

Residuals are errors.  More specifically, they are the differences between the actual value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{actual }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

We can see visually that the indicated point is $2$ units below the least-squares regression line.

![A graph plots Storage life, in days, from 0 to 11, in increments of 1, versus Storage temperature, in degrees Celsius, from 14 to 20, in increments of 1, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (14, 8), (14, 9.5), (15, 10.5), (16, 6), (16, 9.5), (17, 7), (17, 9), (18, 5.5), (18, 7), (19, 4.9), (20, 4.8), and (20, 5.5). A line of regression falls through (10, 11), (16, 8), and (20, 5). A red arrow, labeled negative 2, points vertically downward from the line to (16, 6). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f02398e39278080541d82c7f059329129d46f2da)

This vertical distance describes how far away the actual storage life was from this bunch of bananas' predicted storage life.

Since the point is below the line, the bunch's storage life was lower than the line predicted based on the storage temperature.
This bunch's storage life was $2$ fewer days than predicted based on the storage temperature.

choices.content: This bunch's storage temperature was $2^\circ\text{C}$ warmer than predicted based on the storage life.
This bunch's storage temperature was $2^\circ\text{C}$ cooler than predicted based on the storage life.
This bunch's storage life was $2$ more days than predicted based on the storage temperature.
This bunch's storage life was $2$ fewer days than predicted based on the storage temperature.

assessment_items.question_content:
Chloe surveyed students in her class about the numbers of text messages they sent and received the previous day. An approximate least-squares regression line was used to predict the number of text messages received from a given number of text messages sent.

![A graph plots Text messages received, from 0 to 80, in increments of 5, versus Text messages sent, from 0 to 80, in increments of 5, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (0, 0), (4, 4), (10, 20), (20, 25), (20, 30), (30, 35), (40, 40), (40, 45), (42, 40), (50, 60), (55, 80) and (80, 80). A line of regression rises through (5, 10), (40, 45), and (70, 75). A green arrow, points vertically upward from the line to (55, 80). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e005b3b2464c0017415bbb6b8ca236bd8c4624f5)

**Interpret the residual for the student indicated in the scatterplot above.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##What is a residual?

Residuals are errors.  More specifically, they are the differences between the actual value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{actual }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

We can see visually that the indicated point is $20$ units above the least-squares regression line.

![A graph plots Text messages received, from 0 to 80, in increments of 5, versus Text messages sent, from 0 to 80, in increments of 5, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (0, 0), (4, 4), (10, 20), (20, 25), (20, 30), (30, 35), (40, 40), (40, 45), (42, 40), (50, 60), (55, 80) and (80, 80). A line of regression rises through (5, 10), (40, 45), and (70, 75). A green arrow, labeled 20, points vertically upward from the line to (55, 80). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/905d37fb06ca3a22dacc3b23ca1889c177d6445b)

This vertical distance describes how far away the actual number of text messages received was from this student's predicted number of text messages received.

Since the point is above the line, the number of text messages the student received was greater than the line predicted based on the number of text messages the student sent.
This student received $20$ more text messages than predicted based on the number of text messages sent.

choices.content: This student received $20$ more text messages than predicted based on the number of text messages sent.
This student received $20$ fewer text messages than predicted based on the number of text messages sent.
This student sent $20$ more text messages than predicted based on the number of text messages received.
This student sent $20$ fewer text messages than predicted based on the number of text messages received.

assessment_items.question_content:
Noah tested several men of different ages to see how many push ups they could complete in a row without resting. An approximate least-squares regression line was used to predict the number of push ups from a given age of man.

![A graph plots Number of push ups, from 0 to 30, in increments of 2, versus Age, in years, from 20 to 60 in increments of 5, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (20, 37), (25, 33), (30, 18), (35, 27), (40, 24), (45, 21), (50, 13), (55, 15), (60, 15), and (65, 18). A line of regression falls through (20, 32), (30, 28), and (60, 16). A green arrow points vertically downward from the line to (30, 18). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1e767ad35ee21f27bcd31c14ae21f6313ef69f28)

**Interpret the residual for the man indicated in the scatterplot above.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##What is a residual?

Residuals are errors.  More specifically, they are the differences between the actual value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{actual }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

We can see visually that the indicated point is $10$ units below the least-squares regression line.

![A graph plots Number of push ups, from 0 to 30, in increments of 2, versus Age, in years, from 20 to 60 in increments of 5, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (20, 37), (25, 33), (30, 18), (35, 27), (40, 24), (45, 21), (50, 13), (55, 15), (60, 15), and (65, 18). A line of regression falls through (20, 32), (30, 28), and (60, 16). A red arrow, labeled negative 10, points vertically downward from the line to (30, 18). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fd1578a127aaf048ead4c9062d81bf28b875e6ce)

This vertical distance describes how far away the actual number of completed push ups was from this man's predicted number of push ups.

Since the point is below the line, the number of push ups the man completed was less than the line predicted based on the man's age.
This man completed $10$ fewer push ups than predicted based on his age.

choices.content: This man was $10$ years older than predicted based on the number of push ups he completed.
This man was $10$ years younger than predicted based on the number of push ups he completed.
This man completed $10$ more push ups than predicted based on his age.
This man completed $10$ fewer push ups than predicted based on his age.

assessment_items.question_content:
An insurance actuary tracks the customer mass and the dosage of a certain medicine prescribed to customers. An approximate least-squares regression line was used to predict the dosage, in milligrams, from a given customer mass, in kilograms.

![A graph plots Dosage, in milligrams, from 0 to 600, in increments of 25, versus Mass, in kilograms, from 0 to 70, in increments of 5, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (25, 160), (28, 200), (34, 340), (34, 350), (41, 325), (51, 370), (53, 325), (53, 390), (57, 450), (70, 475), and (75, 590). A line of regression rises through (0, 0), (20, 150), and (60, 450). A green arrow, points vertically downward from the line to (70, 475). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1e3525deaa489c9f870ab4bb8edf4bed33f416b5)

**Interpret the residual for the customer indicated in the scatterplot above.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##What is a residual?

Residuals are errors.  More specifically, they are the differences between the actual value of the response variable and the value predicted by the least squares regression line.

$\text{residual}=(\text{actual }y)-(\text{predicted }y)$

or

$\text{residual}=y-\hat y$

We can see visually that the indicated point is $50$ units below the least-squares regression line.

![A graph plots Dosage, in milligrams, from 0 to 600, in increments of 25, versus Mass, in kilograms, from 0 to 70, in increments of 5, on an x y plane. Points are plotted as follows: (25, 160), (28, 200), (34, 340), (34, 350), (41, 325), (51, 370), (53, 325), (53, 390), (57, 450), (70, 475), and (75, 590). A line of regression rises through (0, 0), (20, 150), and (60, 450). A red arrow, labeled negative 50, points vertically downward from the line to (70, 475). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b22995bfc8b857c4d6914f8cdd536c05eed380cf)

This vertical distance describes how far away the actual dosage was from this customer's predicted dosage.

Since the point is below the line, the customer's dosage was less than the line predicted based on the customer's mass.
This customer was prescribed a dosage $50\,\text{mg}$ lower than predicted based on the customer's mass.

choices.content: This customer was prescribed a dosage $50\,\text{mg}$ higher than predicted based on the customer's mass.
This customer was prescribed a dosage $50\,\text{mg}$ lower than predicted based on the customer's mass.
This customer has a mass $50\,\text{kg}$ greater than predicted by the prescribed dosage.
This customer has a mass $50\,\text{kg}$ lower than predicted by the prescribed dosage.
